title: A Career Pivot from Law to Carpentry
summary: A journalist interviews a former corporate lawyer who left a successful legal career to become a furniture maker, exploring what drove the change, what was lost, and what was gained.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: You were a corporate lawyer for twelve years. What made you decide to leave all of that?

body.speaker: Subject
body.text: It wasn't a single moment. It built up gradually. By year ten I had started asking myself whether I was good at my job because I genuinely cared about the work, or just because I was competent and it paid well. Those are different things.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: Did you enjoy law at the beginning?

body.speaker: Subject
body.text: I did. The first few years were genuinely interesting. I was learning constantly, every case was different, and I had a sense of building something — a career, a reputation, a skill set. By year seven or eight, that feeling had gone. The work had become predictable, even though it was still demanding.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: When did carpentry enter the picture?

body.speaker: Subject
body.text: About five years before I left. My wife and I had just moved into an older house and we wanted some bookshelves. I looked at the quotes we were getting and thought, how difficult could this actually be? I bought a few tools, made something very rough, and then spent a month trying to understand why it wasn't better. I was completely absorbed.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: What was different about that experience compared to law?

body.speaker: Subject
body.text: At the end of the day, there was something physical I could point to. In law, you produce documents, you advise people, and most of what you have contributed is invisible. If you're lucky, a deal closes or a case is settled, and even then the result belongs to someone else. With a piece of furniture, the thing exists, it has weight, you can run your hand across the surface and tell immediately whether you have done it well.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: A lot of people have hobbies that give them that kind of satisfaction. Why did you make it your career?

body.speaker: Subject
body.text: Because hobbies let you stay comfortable. You do it when you feel like it, you accept a standard that's good enough for an amateur, and nothing is at stake. I wanted to be pushed. I had this sense that if I kept carpentry as a hobby, I would never find out what I was actually capable of. The only way to know was to do it seriously.

body.speaker: Subject
body.text: A large one. My wife and I sat down and calculated what we needed to survive, not comfortably, but survive. We had savings that could cover two years. After that, it needed to work. That deadline was actually useful. It meant I couldn't be too precious about it. I had to price the work properly, find customers, and treat it like a business from the beginning.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: Did the legal background help at all?

body.speaker: Subject
body.text: More than I expected. Contracts, disputes with suppliers, understanding what my clients actually wanted versus what they were saying they wanted — that's all negotiation and interpretation. The skills transferred. What didn't transfer was the status. People who had previously called me a barrister were now calling me a carpenter. For a while, that stung.

body.speaker: Subject
body.text: I miss the colleagues and I miss the intellectual challenge of a complicated case. I don't miss the feeling of spending my best hours on something that didn't matter much to me. That might sound ungrateful, given what the career gave me. But it's the truth.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: What would you say to someone in a similar position — a successful professional who is thinking about a radical change?

body.speaker: Subject
body.text: I'd say: make sure you have actually tried the thing you want to move to. Not imagined it, not read about it — tried it. And be honest about whether the problem is the career or the current job. Sometimes people leave a profession when they actually just need to leave a particular firm or manager. That's a much easier fix. But if you've honestly examined that and the answer is still the career itself, then the longer you wait, the harder the change becomes. The identity gets more rigid as you get older.

annotations.meaningVi: chuyển hướng nghề nghiệp, thay đổi ngành
annotations.exampleEn: His career pivot from law to carpentry surprised everyone.
The dissatisfaction didn't happen overnight — it built up gradually.

annotations.meaningVi: cảm giác đang xây dựng điều gì đó
annotations.exampleEn: In the early years, I had a sense of building something.
I was completely absorbed in trying to make a better shelf.
Most of what lawyers contribute is invisible — documents and advice.

annotations.meaningVi: bị đặt cược, có nguy cơ mất
annotations.exampleEn: In a hobby, nothing is at stake. In a career, everything is.

annotations.meaningVi: quá cẩn thận / cầu kỳ đến mức không dám làm
annotations.exampleEn: The deadline meant I couldn't be too precious about the work.

annotations.meaningVi: định giá công việc đúng cách
annotations.exampleEn: I had to price the work properly and treat it like a business.
Understanding what clients want is negotiation and interpretation.

annotations.meaningVi: các kỹ năng được áp dụng sang lĩnh vực mới
annotations.exampleEn: The legal skills transferred more than I expected.

annotations.meaningVi: đau (theo nghĩa cảm xúc), bị tổn thương
annotations.exampleEn: Losing the title of barrister stung for a while.
I miss the intellectual challenge of a complicated case.

annotations.meaningVi: dành những giờ tốt nhất của mình cho
annotations.exampleEn: I don't miss spending my best hours on something that didn't matter to me.

annotations.meaningVi: thay đổi triệt để, thay đổi căn bản
annotations.exampleEn: What advice would you give to someone considering a radical change?

annotations.meaningVi: bản sắc nghề nghiệp trở nên cứng nhắc hơn
annotations.exampleEn: The longer you wait, the more rigid your professional identity becomes.

annotations.meaningVi: đã xem xét điều đó kỹ lưỡng
annotations.exampleEn: If you've honestly examined that and the answer is still the career itself.

grammarNotes.title: Past Perfect for Describing States That Changed
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Nhân vật dùng quá khứ hoàn thành để mô tả các trạng thái đã thay đổi trước một điểm trong quá khứ: 'By year seven or eight, that feeling had gone', 'I had started asking myself whether I was good at my job', 'People who had previously called me a barrister were now calling me a carpenter'. Công thức: had + V3. Nhấn mạnh rằng một trạng thái hoặc hành động đã hoàn thành trước một thời điểm tham chiếu khác trong quá khứ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The subject uses past perfect to describe states that had changed before a point in the past: 'By year seven or eight, that feeling had gone', 'I had started asking myself', 'People who had previously called me a barrister were now calling me a carpenter'. Form: had + past participle. Emphasises that a state or action was completed before another past reference point.

grammarNotes.title: Reported Speech with Modal Verbs
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Người được phỏng vấn dùng lời nói tường thuật để diễn tả điều họ từng nghĩ hoặc nói với bản thân: 'asking myself whether I was good at my job or just because I was competent', 'I thought, how difficult could this actually be?', 'I had this sense that if I kept carpentry as a hobby, I would never find out what I was actually capable of'. Cấu trúc: S + thought/wondered/asked myself + mệnh đề với thì lùi. Động từ khuyết thiếu trong lời nói tường thuật: can → could, will → would.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The subject uses reported speech to convey past thoughts: 'asking myself whether I was good at my job', 'I thought, how difficult could this actually be?', 'I had this sense that if I kept carpentry as a hobby, I would never find out what I was capable of'. Form: thought/wondered/asked myself + reported clause with backshifted tense. Modal shifts: can → could, will → would in reported speech.

translationVi:
Người phỏng vấn: Anh đã là luật sư công ty trong mười hai năm. Điều gì khiến anh quyết định bỏ tất cả?

Nhân vật: Không phải một khoảnh khắc duy nhất. Nó tích lũy dần dần. Đến năm thứ mười tôi đã bắt đầu tự hỏi liệu tôi có giỏi công việc vì tôi thực sự quan tâm đến nó, hay chỉ vì tôi có năng lực và nó trả lương tốt. Đó là hai điều khác nhau.

Người phỏng vấn: Anh có thích luật ban đầu không?

Nhân vật: Có. Một vài năm đầu thực sự thú vị. Tôi liên tục học hỏi, mỗi vụ án đều khác nhau, và tôi có cảm giác đang xây dựng điều gì đó — một sự nghiệp, danh tiếng, một bộ kỹ năng. Đến năm bảy hay tám, cảm giác đó đã biến mất. Công việc đã trở nên có thể đoán trước, dù vẫn còn đòi hỏi.

Người phỏng vấn: Khi nào mộc nghiệp xuất hiện?

Nhân vật: Khoảng năm năm trước khi tôi rời đi. Vợ tôi và tôi vừa chuyển vào một ngôi nhà cũ hơn và chúng tôi muốn một số kệ sách. Tôi xem qua các báo giá chúng tôi nhận được và nghĩ, điều này thực sự khó đến mức nào? Tôi mua một vài dụng cụ, làm được thứ gì đó rất thô ráp, và sau đó dành một tháng cố gắng hiểu tại sao nó không tốt hơn. Tôi bị cuốn hút hoàn toàn.

Người phỏng vấn: Điều đó khác với luật như thế nào?

Nhân vật: Cuối ngày, có thứ gì đó hữu hình tôi có thể chỉ vào. Trong luật, bạn tạo ra các tài liệu, bạn tư vấn cho mọi người, và hầu hết những gì bạn đóng góp là vô hình. Nếu may mắn, một thỏa thuận được ký kết hoặc một vụ kiện được dàn xếp, và ngay cả khi đó kết quả thuộc về người khác. Với một món đồ nội thất, thứ đó tồn tại, nó có trọng lượng, bạn có thể chạy tay qua bề mặt và biết ngay lập tức liệu bạn có làm tốt không.

Người phỏng vấn: Nhiều người có sở thích cho họ loại sự thỏa mãn đó. Tại sao anh lại biến nó thành nghề nghiệp?

Nhân vật: Vì sở thích cho phép bạn ở thoải mái. Bạn làm nó khi bạn cảm thấy muốn, bạn chấp nhận một tiêu chuẩn đủ tốt cho nghiệp dư, và không có gì bị đặt cược. Tôi muốn bị thách thức. Tôi có cảm giác rằng nếu tôi giữ mộc nghiệp là sở thích, tôi sẽ không bao giờ biết mình thực sự có khả năng gì. Cách duy nhất để biết là làm nghiêm túc.

Người phỏng vấn: Có rủi ro tài chính không?

Nhân vật: Rủi ro lớn. Vợ tôi và tôi ngồi xuống và tính toán những gì chúng tôi cần để sống sót, không phải thoải mái, mà là sống sót. Chúng tôi có tiết kiệm có thể trang trải hai năm. Sau đó, nó cần phải hoạt động. Thời hạn đó thực sự hữu ích. Nó có nghĩa là tôi không thể quá cẩn thận về nó. Tôi phải định giá công việc đúng cách, tìm khách hàng và coi nó như một doanh nghiệp ngay từ đầu.

Người phỏng vấn: Nền tảng pháp lý có giúp ích không?

Nhân vật: Nhiều hơn tôi mong đợi. Hợp đồng, tranh chấp với nhà cung cấp, hiểu những gì khách hàng thực sự muốn so với những gì họ nói họ muốn — đó là tất cả đàm phán và diễn giải. Các kỹ năng đã được chuyển sang. Điều không chuyển sang được là địa vị. Những người trước đây gọi tôi là luật sư bào chữa giờ đang gọi tôi là thợ mộc. Một thời gian, điều đó đau đớn.

Người phỏng vấn: Anh có nhớ luật không?

Nhân vật: Tôi nhớ các đồng nghiệp và tôi nhớ thách thức trí tuệ của một vụ án phức tạp. Tôi không nhớ cảm giác dành những giờ tốt nhất của mình cho điều gì đó không quan trọng lắm với tôi. Điều đó có thể nghe vô ơn, khi nhớ đến những gì sự nghiệp đã cho tôi. Nhưng đó là sự thật.

Người phỏng vấn: Anh sẽ nói gì với ai đó trong tình huống tương tự — một chuyên gia thành công đang nghĩ đến một sự thay đổi triệt để?

Nhân vật: Tôi sẽ nói: hãy chắc chắn rằng bạn đã thực sự thử thứ bạn muốn chuyển sang. Không phải tưởng tượng, không phải đọc về nó — thử nó. Và hãy thành thật về việc vấn đề là nghề nghiệp hay công việc hiện tại. Đôi khi mọi người rời bỏ một nghề trong khi thực ra họ chỉ cần rời khỏi một công ty hoặc người quản lý cụ thể. Đó là một giải pháp dễ dàng hơn nhiều. Nhưng nếu bạn đã thực sự xem xét điều đó và câu trả lời vẫn là nghề nghiệp, thì càng chờ đợi lâu, sự thay đổi càng trở nên khó hơn. Bản sắc trở nên cứng nhắc hơn khi bạn già đi.

questions.prompt: How does the subject describe his decision to leave law?
options: It was triggered by a single bad experience with a client
It built up gradually over years of growing dissatisfaction
He was made redundant and took the opportunity to change
His wife persuaded him to leave
questions.explanation: The subject says 'It wasn't a single moment. It built up gradually.'
questions.hint: The subject contrasts his experience with the idea of a single turning point.

questions.prompt: What did the subject enjoy about law in the early years?
options: The high salary and professional status
Working with famous clients on high-profile cases
Constant learning, variety, and a sense of building a career
The short hours and good work-life balance
questions.explanation: The subject says 'I was learning constantly, every case was different, and I had a sense of building something — a career, a reputation, a skill set.'
questions.hint: Three positive aspects of the early years are listed.

questions.prompt: What first led the subject to try carpentry?
options: He attended a weekend woodworking course
He wanted bookshelves for a new house and decided to make them himself
He inherited tools from a relative who was a carpenter
He read an article about craftsmen who had left professional jobs
questions.explanation: The subject says 'My wife and I had just moved into an older house and we wanted some bookshelves. I looked at the quotes... and thought, how difficult could this actually be?'
questions.hint: A practical household need was the trigger.

questions.prompt: What did the subject find most satisfying about carpentry compared to law?
options: It was less stressful and better paid
The physical end result — something he could touch and evaluate himself
The social connection with other craftspeople
Working outdoors rather than in an office
questions.explanation: The subject says 'At the end of the day, there was something physical I could point to... you can run your hand across the surface and tell immediately whether you have done it well.'
questions.hint: The key contrast is between visible/physical and invisible work.

questions.prompt: Why did the subject decide to make carpentry his career rather than keeping it as a hobby?
options: He thought it would earn him more money than law
He felt that hobbies kept you comfortable and he wanted to discover what he was truly capable of
He was offered a commission that was too good to refuse
His wife wanted him to spend more time at home
questions.explanation: The subject says 'hobbies let you stay comfortable... I wanted to be pushed. I had this sense that if I kept carpentry as a hobby, I would never find out what I was actually capable of.'
questions.hint: Discomfort and discovering capability are both in the answer.

questions.prompt: How did the subject and his wife manage the financial risk?
options: They took out a loan to cover living costs
They calculated a survival budget and used two years of savings as a runway
The wife continued working while he transitioned gradually
They sold the house and moved to a cheaper area
questions.explanation: The subject says 'We calculated what we needed to survive... We had savings that could cover two years. After that, it needed to work.'
questions.hint: A specific time period and financial plan are both mentioned.

questions.prompt: Why did the subject find the financial deadline useful?
options: It helped him get bank loans more easily
It meant he could not be too precious about the work and had to treat it like a business
It encouraged him to take on more ambitious projects
It gave him a clear date to tell his law firm he was leaving
questions.explanation: The subject says 'That deadline was actually useful. It meant I couldn't be too precious about it. I had to price the work properly... and treat it like a business from the beginning.'
questions.hint: Two practical consequences of the deadline are described.

questions.prompt: Which legal skills transferred to carpentry?
options: Understanding contracts, handling supplier disputes, and interpreting what clients actually wanted
Writing detailed reports and legal documents
questions.explanation: The subject says 'Contracts, disputes with suppliers, understanding what my clients actually wanted versus what they were saying they wanted — that's all negotiation and interpretation.'
questions.hint: Three specific transferable skills are named.

questions.prompt: What did NOT transfer from law to carpentry?
options: Status — people now called him a carpenter rather than a barrister
questions.explanation: The subject says 'What didn't transfer was the status. People who had previously called me a barrister were now calling me a carpenter. For a while, that stung.'
questions.hint: Only one thing is explicitly said not to have transferred.

questions.prompt: What does the subject miss about law?
options: The money and the office environment
The colleagues and the intellectual challenge of complicated cases
The predictability of a well-structured career path
The social recognition that came with the title
questions.explanation: The subject says 'I miss the colleagues and I miss the intellectual challenge of a complicated case.'
questions.hint: Two things are explicitly said to be missed.

questions.prompt: What is the subject's first piece of advice for someone considering a career change?
options: Make sure you have enough savings before you leave
Talk to people who have already made the change
Make sure you have actually tried the new thing, not just imagined it
Be prepared for your status to drop significantly
questions.explanation: The subject says 'make sure you have actually tried the thing you want to move to. Not imagined it, not read about it — tried it.'
questions.hint: The emphasis is on direct experience, not research or planning.

questions.prompt: What does the subject mean by 'the identity gets more rigid as you get older'?
options: Older professionals are less creative and adaptable
The longer you stay in a career, the harder it becomes to see yourself differently and change
Physical limitations make it harder to learn new skills
Employers are less willing to hire older career changers
questions.explanation: The subject warns that delaying a change makes it harder because your professional identity becomes more fixed — it's harder to let go of how you define yourself.
questions.hint: 'Rigid' means fixed and inflexible — apply that to how we see ourselves.

cloze.template:
Interviewer: You were a corporate lawyer for twelve years. What made you decide to leave?
Subject: It wasn't a single moment. It {{b1}} up gradually. By year ten I had started asking myself whether I was good at my job because I genuinely cared, or just because I was {{b2}} and it paid well.
Interviewer: When did carpentry enter the picture?
Subject: About five years before I left. I wanted some bookshelves and thought, how difficult could this actually be? I made something very rough and spent a month trying to understand why it wasn't better. I was completely {{b3}}.
Interviewer: What was different compared to law?
Subject: At the end of the day, there was something physical I could point to. In law, most of what you contribute is {{b4}}. With a piece of furniture, you can run your hand across the surface and tell immediately whether you've done it well.
Interviewer: Why did you make it your career rather than a hobby?
Subject: Because hobbies let you stay {{b5}}. Nothing is at stake. I wanted to find out what I was actually capable of, and the only way was to do it seriously.
Interviewer: Was there a financial risk?
Subject: A large one. We calculated what we needed to survive and had savings to cover two years. That deadline was useful — it meant I couldn't be too {{b6}} about it. I had to {{b7}} the work properly and treat it like a business.
Interviewer: Did the legal background help?
Subject: More than I expected. The skills {{b8}}. What didn't transfer was the status. People who had previously called me a barrister were now calling me a carpenter. For a while, that {{b9}}.
Interviewer: What would you say to someone considering a radical change?
Subject: Make sure you have actually tried the thing — not imagined it, not read about it. And be honest about whether the problem is the career or just the current job. The longer you wait, the harder the change becomes. The {{b10}} gets more rigid as you get older.

blanks.explanation: 'Built up gradually' is the exact phrasal verb — to build up means to accumulate over time.
'Competent' is the exact word — it means able to do something adequately, but with a slightly clinical, detached quality compared to 'talented'.
'Completely absorbed' is the exact phrase — absorbed means fully immersed in something to the exclusion of everything else.
'Invisible' is the exact word used — most legal work cannot be pointed to or seen as a physical object.
'Hobbies let you stay comfortable' is the exact phrase — comfortable here implies no risk and no pressure to improve.
'Too precious about it' is the exact idiomatic phrase — to be too precious means to be overly protective or fussy in a way that prevents action.
'Price the work properly' is the exact phrase — to price means to set the fee or selling price, which is a specific business decision.
'The skills transferred' is the exact phrase — to transfer means to move effectively from one context to another.
'That stung' is the exact colloquial past tense — to sting emotionally means to cause a sharp, painful feeling of hurt or wounded pride.
'The identity gets more rigid' is the exact phrase — professional identity specifically refers to how you define yourself through your career.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The subject says 'the identity gets more rigid as you get older.' Do you think professional identity is something that protects people or limits them? How much of who you are should be connected to what you do for work?

What didn't transfer was the status. People who had previously called me a barrister were now calling me a carpenter. For a while, that stung.

sentences.speaker: Subject
sentences.text: I miss the colleagues and I miss the intellectual challenge of a complicated case. I don't miss the feeling of spending my best hours on something that didn't matter much to me. That might sound ungrateful, given what the career gave me. But it's the truth.

sentences.speaker: Interviewer
sentences.text: What would you say to someone in a similar position — a successful professional who is thinking about a radical change?

sentences.speaker: Subject
sentences.text: I'd say: make sure you have actually tried the thing you want to move to. Not imagined it, not read about it — tried it. And be honest about whether the problem is the career or the current job. Sometimes people leave a profession when they actually just need to leave a particular firm or manager. That's a much easier fix. But if you've honestly examined that and the answer is still the career itself, then the longer you wait, the harder the change becomes. The identity gets more rigid as you get older.
